title: P1: Core types and enums module
description:
## Task
Define all core types and enums that the rest of the codebase depends on.
These types enforce exhaustive matching — the compiler guarantees every
variant is handled. This is the primary defense against the undefined-path
bugs that caused v1 failures.

## Background
v1 had no type system. Exit codes were checked with ad-hoc if/else chains,
outcomes were implicit, and missing handlers were invisible until runtime.
The types module makes every possible state and outcome explicit and
compiler-enforced. If a new variant is added, every match site must be
updated or the build fails.

## Deliverables

### State enum
BOOTING, SELECTING, CLAIMING, RETRYING, BUILDING, DISPATCHING, EXECUTING,
HANDLING, LOGGING, EXHAUSTED, STOPPED, ERRORED

### Outcome enum
Success, Failure, Timeout, Crash(i32), AgentNotFound, Interrupted

### StrandResult enum
BeadFound(Vec<Bead>), WorkCreated, NoWork, Error(StrandError)

### ClaimResult enum
Claimed(Bead), RaceLost { claimed_by: String }, NotClaimable { reason: String }

### ClaimOutcome enum
Claimed(Bead), AllRaceLost, NoCandidates, StoreError(Error)

### Bead struct
id, title, body, priority, status, assignee, labels, workspace, dependencies,
created_at, updated_at

### BeadId newtype
Wraps String with Display, FromStr, Hash, Eq impls.

### Error types
NeedleError enum with variants for each Tier (Transient, BeadScoped, WorkerScoped).
Implement std::error::Error. Include context (which bead, which workspace, etc.).

### InputMethod enum
Stdin, File { path_template: String }, Args { flag: String }

### PeerStatus enum
Alive { last_seen, current_bead }, Stale { last_seen, claimed_bead }, Dead { heartbeat_file }

## Acceptance Criteria
- All enums derive Debug, Clone, PartialEq at minimum
- All enums have #[non_exhaustive] where future variants are expected
- Bead struct implements serde::Deserialize for JSON parsing from br output
- BeadId implements all comparison/hash traits
- NeedleError has Display impl with human-readable messages
- Unit tests verify serialization/deserialization roundtrips
- No wildcard (_) matches anywhere — all arms are explicit

## Considerations
- These types are the contract between all modules. Changes here ripple
  everywhere, so get them right. Prefer adding optional fields (Option<T>)
  over breaking changes.
- The Outcome enum must NOT have a catch-all. The classify() function maps
  every exit code range to a specific variant. Unknown codes map to Failure
  explicitly (not via _).
close_reason: Implemented all core types: BeadId newtype, Bead struct, WorkerState/Outcome/StrandResult/ClaimResult/ClaimOutcome/InputMethod/PeerStatus enums, ProcessOutput struct, NeedleError with ErrorTier, WorkerId type alias. 9 serde roundtrip and classify unit tests pass. Updated all modules to use new types. cargo build/test/clippy all clean.

title: Worker launch silently fails when NATO session name already exists in tmux
description: When launching workers, NEEDLE always starts from 'alpha' in the NATO alphabet. If a tmux session named 'alpha' already exists (from a previous run or different system), the new worker's agent gets dispatched into a conflicting session, exits with code -1 (interrupted), and the worker stops with 0 beads processed. No error is logged — it silently fails. Fix: before assigning a NATO name, check tmux list-sessions and skip names that are already taken. Use the next available name in the sequence. DO NOT split this bead.
close_reason: Fix verified: occupied_worker_ids() checks tmux sessions, skips taken NATO names. NEEDLE_INNER=1 replaces is_inside_tmux(). All unit + E2E tests pass.

title: P1: Outcome handler with exhaustive exit code classification
description:
## Task
Classify agent exit codes into typed Outcome variants and execute the
explicit handler for each. Every exit code maps to exactly one outcome.
There are no unhandled cases.

## Background
This is the core of Principle 2 (explicit outcome paths). v1 had ad-hoc
exit code checking scattered across multiple functions with inconsistent
handling. Some exit codes were silently ignored. v2 routes all exits through
a single classify() function that returns an Outcome enum, and a single
handle() function that pattern-matches on all variants.

## Deliverables

### classify() function
Input: ExecutionResult, was_interrupted (bool)
Output: Outcome

Mapping:
- was_interrupted → Outcome::Interrupted
- exit 0 → Outcome::Success
- exit 1 → Outcome::Failure
- exit 124 → Outcome::Timeout
- exit 127 → Outcome::AgentNotFound
- exit >128 → Outcome::Crash(exit_code)
- any other → Outcome::Failure (explicit mapping, not _ wildcard)

### handle() function
Input: Outcome, claimed_bead (Bead), bead_store (&dyn BeadStore)
Output: Result<HandlerResult>

Actions per outcome:
- Success: Check if bead was closed by agent (br show, check status=closed).
  If still open, emit bead.orphaned warning. Do NOT auto-close.
- Failure: Evaluate for mitosis (Phase 2, stub in Phase 1). If not splittable,
  release bead (br update --status open --unassign). Increment failure count label.
- Timeout: Release bead. Add deferred label.
- Crash: Release bead. Create alert bead in workspace.
- AgentNotFound: Release bead. Emit error. (No retry — config issue.)
- Interrupted: Release bead. (Graceful shutdown path.)

### HandlerResult struct
outcome (Outcome), bead_action (Released | Deferred | Alerted | None),
telemetry_events (Vec<TelemetryEvent>)

## Acceptance Criteria
- classify() has NO wildcard match arm — every exit code range is explicit
- handle() has NO wildcard match arm — every Outcome variant is handled
- Success + bead still open → warning emitted (not auto-closed)
- Failure → bead released and failure count incremented
- Timeout → bead released + deferred label added
- Crash → bead released + alert bead created
- AgentNotFound → bead released + no retry
- All handler actions produce telemetry events
- Adding a new Outcome variant causes a compile error at every match site

## Considerations
- The mitosis evaluation in the Failure handler is a stub in Phase 1 —
  it always returns "not splittable" and falls through to normal release.
  Phase 2 implements the actual mitosis logic.
- The alert bead creation on Crash should include diagnostic info: agent
  name, exit code (signal number), bead ID, workspace, timestamp.
close_reason: Fully implemented: exhaustive exit code classification, all outcome handlers with telemetry, 15 unit tests passing

title: P2: Mitosis — bead splitting with child-aware deduplication
description:
## Task
Split multi-task beads into focused children on first failure. Use the
parent's existing children as the dedup source — structurally preventing
duplicate splits.

## Background
v1 mitosis created 5,741 duplicate beads because: (1) br show --json omitted
labels so all guards failed silently, (2) no depth limit, (3) no dedup,
(4) concurrent workers split the same bead simultaneously. v2 solves all
four by checking the parent's existing children before creating new ones.

## Split criteria (semantic, not numeric)
A bead should split when it describes MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT TASKS. The agent
analyzes the bead and answers: "Does this describe >1 independent unit of work?"
Valid: "add endpoint AND write migration AND update tests" (three deliverables)
Invalid: "the bead is long" or "the bead failed" (not composite reasons)

## Child-aware dedup algorithm
1. Read parent's existing children (dependencies)
2. For each proposed child from agent:
   a. Does an existing child cover this task? → Skip
   b. Novel task? → Create child, link as blocking parent
3. If all children already existed → no-op (split already happened)

## Concurrency
- Workspace flock held for entire mitosis (read children → create → update deps)
- Serializes mitosis across workers for same parent
- Second worker enters flock, sees children from first, skips all

## Deliverables
- Mitosis evaluator: agent dispatch with mitosis prompt template
- Child dedup: compare proposed vs existing children
- Child creation: br create for each novel child + br dep add
- Parent blocking: parent remains blocked by children
- Config: mitosis.enabled (default true), mitosis.first_failure_only (default true)
- Telemetry: bead.mitosis.evaluated, bead.mitosis.split, bead.mitosis.skipped

## Acceptance Criteria
- Multi-task bead splits into correct children on first failure
- Single-task bead is NOT split (released for retry instead)
- Second mitosis on same parent creates zero new children (dedup verified)
- Two concurrent workers: flock serializes, no duplicates
- Parent blocked until all children complete
- Only triggers on first failure (not retries)
close_reason: done

title: E2E: Agent adapter invocation with real claude CLI
description:
## What

Validate that the Dispatcher correctly invokes a real agent CLI binary using the adapter YAML config. This tests the full invocation chain: template rendering, env var injection, heredoc prompt delivery, process management, and stream-json output capture.

## Test Plan

Create a test agent adapter that uses a mock script instead of claude:

```yaml
name: test-mock-claude
invoke: |
  cd ${WORKSPACE} && \
  echo '{"type":"result","result":"done","cost_usd":0.001,"usage":{"input_tokens":100,"output_tokens":50}}' && \
  br close ${BEAD_ID} --body "Mock agent completed"
```

Then test with `needle run` using this adapter and verify:

1. **Template variables** are correctly substituted: ${WORKSPACE}, ${PROMPT}, ${BEAD_ID}, ${BEAD_TITLE}, ${AGENT_DIR}
2. **Environment variables** from the adapter config are set in the child process
3. **Heredoc prompt** is correctly passed (no shell escaping issues with special chars, newlines, backticks)
4. **Process timeout** kills the agent if it exceeds configured timeout
5. **Exit codes** are correctly classified: 0=success, 1=retry, 2/137=fail
6. **Output capture** works for stream-json format

## Edge Cases to Test
- Bead body containing shell metacharacters ($, `, ', ", \, newlines)
- Agent adapter with ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL and custom env vars
- Timeout kill (agent sleeps longer than timeout)
- Agent exits with code 1 (retry path)
- Agent exits with code 137 (SIGKILL / OOM path)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 6 verification points pass
- Edge cases don't cause shell injection or prompt corruption
- Tests run without any real API calls
close_reason: Implemented 12 E2E dispatch tests covering full agent adapter invocation chain: template variables, env vars, shell metacharacter safety, exit code classification, timeout enforcement, JSON output capture, and workspace verification.

title: E2E: Single worker full bead lifecycle
description:
## What

End-to-end test that proves the complete bead lifecycle works with a real `br` workspace and a real (trivial) agent.

## Setup

Create a test harness script (tests/e2e/single_worker.sh) that:

1. Creates a temp directory with a `.beads/` workspace (`br init`)
2. Creates a simple test bead: `br create --type task --title "Create a file called DONE"`
3. Creates a minimal test agent adapter YAML that runs `echo "done" > DONE && br close <bead_id>` (no real LLM needed)
4. Runs `needle run --workspace <tmpdir> --agent test-echo --count 1`
5. Waits for the worker to reach EXHAUSTED state (max 30s timeout)
6. Asserts:
   - The bead is now CLOSED (`br show <id>` status == closed)
   - The DONE file exists
   - Telemetry log contains: worker.started, bead.claimed, prompt.built, agent.dispatched, bead.closed, worker.exhausted
   - Exit code is 0
7. Cleans up temp directory

## Why

This is the fundamental contract: claim → build prompt → dispatch → agent closes bead → worker moves on. If this doesn't work, nothing works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test script is executable and self-contained (no external dependencies beyond br + needle binary)
- Passes on a clean run with no pre-existing state
- Runs in under 30 seconds
- Documents the exact telemetry event sequence expected
close_reason: E2E single worker lifecycle test implemented in tests/e2e/single_worker.sh. Shell script creates isolated workspace, bead, mock adapter, runs needle binary, and validates: exit code 0, bead closure, DONE file, and 9 core telemetry events. All assertions pass.

description:

Implement the Mend strand: stale claim cleanup, orphaned lock removal,
dependency link repair, and database health checks.

## Background
Mend runs before Explore in the waterfall because cleaning up the home
workspace may unblock local beads. v1 had stale dependency links that
caused permanent blocking, and an infinite loop where mend returned
success on failed releases.

- Stale claim cleanup (integrates peer monitoring)
- Orphaned lock file removal (flock files > lock_ttl)
- Dependency link repair (closed blockers still on open beads)

title: P2: Peer monitoring — stale claim release and crash cleanup
description:
## Task
Integrate heartbeat-based peer monitoring into the Mend strand. Detect
crashed workers and release their claimed beads.

## Background
When a worker crashes mid-execution, its claimed bead is stuck in_progress.
Without cleanup, the bead is permanently blocked. Peer monitoring (via Mend
strand) detects dead workers by reading their stale heartbeat files, verifying
the PID is dead, and releasing the orphaned claim.

## Deliverables

### Peer check algorithm (in Mend strand)
1. Read all heartbeat files in ~/.needle/state/heartbeats/
2. For each stale heartbeat (last_heartbeat + ttl < now):
   a. Check PID alive (kill -0)
   b. If dead: CRASHED
      - Release claimed bead: br update <bead_id> --status open --unassign
      - Remove heartbeat file
      - Deregister from worker registry
      - Emit peer.crashed telemetry
   c. If alive: STUCK
      - Emit peer.stale warning (do NOT kill or release — may be slow)
3. Return: number of beads released

### Safety rules
- Never release a bead from a worker with a live PID
- Never auto-kill a stuck worker (alert only)
- Only Mend strand performs peer monitoring (no other strand touches it)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Crashed worker's bead released within one Mend evaluation
- Stuck worker (stale heartbeat, live PID) → warning only, bead NOT released
- Healthy worker → no action
- Released bead becomes claimable again (verify with br ready)
- Telemetry events emitted for each detection
close_reason: Implemented peer monitoring module with crash cleanup and stale detection

title: Phase 2: Multi-Worker Fleet
description:
## Phase Overview
Multiple workers operate in the same environment. They coordinate through
shared state, detect failures, and self-heal. Workers roam across workspaces.

## Why This Phase Second
Phase 1 proves the single-worker loop. Phase 2 adds the coordination layer
that makes NEEDLE a fleet orchestrator. The concurrency model (flock, heartbeat,
peer monitoring) is the most complex and bug-prone part of the system — it
caused the majority of v1 failures (thundering herd, stale claims, starvation
false positives, explore strand CPU overload).

## Scope
- ~10 additional source files, ~4,000 additional LOC
- Multi-worker launch with staggered startup
- Per-workspace flock for claim serialization
- Heartbeat system with TTL-based stale detection
- Peer monitoring (Mend strand cleans up crashed workers)
- Strand 2 (Mend) and Strand 3 (Explore)
- Mitosis with child-aware deduplication
- Worker state registry, concurrency limits
- Workspace config (.needle.yaml), env var overrides
- Additional agent adapters (Opus, OpenCode, Codex, Aider)
- Cost tracking, budget enforcement
- CLI extensions (attach, status, config, doctor)
- Database auto-recovery

## Key Risks (from v1 learnings)
- Thundering herd: Prevented by per-workspace flock
- Stale claims: Prevented by heartbeat TTL + Mend strand
- Explore CPU overload: Prevented by explicit workspace config (no find scanning)
- Starvation false positives: Prevented by three-state verification
- Mitosis explosion: Prevented by child-aware dedup
close_reason: All P2 tasks completed: multi-worker launch, heartbeat, peer monitoring, mend/explore strands, mitosis, rate limiting, registry, workspace config, agent adapters, cost tracking, CLI extensions, DB auto-recovery, integration tests.

title: Telemetry BufWriter never flushes on short-lived worker sessions
description:
## Bug

When a worker hits EXHAUSTED quickly (e.g., no claimable beads), the telemetry JSONL file is created but remains 0 bytes. Events are emitted to the mpsc channel and written to a BufWriter<File> inside a tokio::spawn task, but:

1. The few events (~200 bytes) never exceed BufWriter's 8KB threshold
2. `run_worker()` (cli/mod.rs:513) calls `rt.block_on(worker.run())` — when `run()` returns, the Runtime drops
3. Runtime drop cancels all spawned tasks, including the telemetry writer
4. The writer's `flush()` at telemetry/mod.rs:1622 never executes

## Impact

All short-lived sessions produce empty log files. `needle logs` and `needle status` show no data for these workers. Long-running sessions that process beads work by accident — their event volume exceeds the BufWriter threshold so data is auto-flushed.

## Reproduction

```bash
# Launch workers against a workspace with no claimable beads
NEEDLE_INNER=1 needle run --workspace /some/empty/workspace --agent glm --count 1 --identifier test
# Check: log file exists but is 0 bytes
ls -la ~/.needle/logs/test-*.jsonl
```

## Fix options

1. **Explicit shutdown**: Add `Telemetry::shutdown()` that drops the sender (closing the channel) and awaits the writer task's JoinHandle, ensuring flush runs before the runtime drops.
2. **Flush on emit**: Call `flush()` after every write in the file sink (trades throughput for reliability).
3. **Periodic flush**: Add a tokio::time::interval that flushes every N seconds.

Option 1 is the cleanest — the worker already knows when it's done.

## Files

- `src/telemetry/mod.rs:1595-1628` — spawn_writer, flush only on channel close
- `src/cli/mod.rs:504-517` — run_worker, runtime drops after block_on
- `src/worker/mod.rs:156-158` — telemetry.start() but no shutdown

title: E2E: Graceful shutdown — SIGTERM during execution
description:
## What

Prove that sending SIGTERM to a needle worker during active bead execution results in a clean shutdown: the agent process is killed, the bead is released (not left in a claimed state), and the worker exits with state STOPPED.

## Test Plan

1. Create a workspace with 1 bead
2. Create a test agent adapter that sleeps for 60 seconds (simulates long execution)
3. Launch worker, wait for EXECUTING state (check heartbeat or telemetry)
4. Send SIGTERM to the worker process
5. Assert:
   - Worker transitions to STOPPED state (not ERRORED)
   - Agent child process is killed (not left orphaned)
   - Bead is released back to open/unassigned (not left claimed by dead worker)
   - Heartbeat file is cleaned up
   - Worker registry entry is removed
   - Telemetry shows: worker.signal_received, agent.killed, bead.released, worker.stopped
   - Exit code is 0 (clean shutdown)

## Edge Cases
- SIGTERM during CLAIMING (before agent starts)
- SIGTERM during BUILDING (prompt construction)
- Double SIGTERM (second signal forces immediate exit)
- SIGKILL (verify bead can be recovered by Mend strand from a different worker)

## Acceptance Criteria
- No orphaned processes after shutdown
- No stuck beads after shutdown
- Clean exit in all edge cases
close_reason: E2E graceful shutdown test implemented in tests/e2e/graceful_shutdown.sh. Test validates: SIGTERM during agent execution → exit code 0, bead released (not closed), heartbeat cleaned up, 13 assertions including telemetry events for interrupted outcome and bead release.

title: P2: Strand 3 (Explore) — multi-workspace discovery
description:
## Task
Implement the Explore strand: search configured workspaces for claimable
beads when the home workspace and mend have nothing.

## Background
v1 Explore used filesystem scanning (find command) which caused 35+ CPU
load with 40 workers. v2 uses an explicit workspace list from config.
No upward traversal, no dynamic discovery, no permanent relocation.

## Deliverables
- Read workspace list from config (static for session duration)
- For each workspace: check .beads/ exists, query br ready --unassigned --json
- Return candidates from first workspace with work
- Workers process one bead from explored workspace, then return home

## Acceptance Criteria
- Finds beads in configured workspace when home is empty
- Skips workspaces without .beads/ directory
- Returns to home workspace after processing explored bead
- No filesystem scanning (only configured paths)
- Static workspace list (doesn't re-read config mid-session)
close_reason: Explore strand implemented: ExploreConfig, ExploreStrand with workspace discovery, waterfall registration (Pluck→Mend→Explore→Knot), 10 unit tests. All 274 tests pass.

title: P3: Validation gates — pre-closure verification
description:
## Task
Run configurable verification commands after agent exits 0 but before
accepting bead closure. If verification fails, release the bead.

## Background
Inspired by bg-gate (docs/research/bg-gate-validation.md). Agents that
say "done" but leave broken code should not have their closures accepted.

## Deliverables
- Config: verification commands per workspace (.needle.yaml)
  verification: ["cargo test", "cargo clippy -- -D warnings"]
- After agent exits 0, run each command in workspace
- All pass → accept closure (or log orphaned if agent didn't close)
- Any fail → release bead with verification failure as context

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests pass → bead closure accepted
- Tests fail → bead released, failure output in telemetry
- No verification configured → skip (default behavior unchanged)
close_reason: Implemented validation gates: verification commands per workspace, integrated into outcome handler success path, reopen+release on failure, telemetry events, full test coverage

workspace by default), weave-generated label.

- Creates valid beads from actual documentation gaps

title: P1: Dispatcher with agent adapter system
description:
## Task
Load agent adapters from YAML config, render invoke templates, execute agent
processes, capture results. Enforce execution timeout.

## Background
NEEDLE is agent-agnostic. The dispatcher does not know how agents work — it
renders a template, starts a process, waits, and captures the result. Adding
a new agent requires only a YAML file. v1 had hardcoded invocation logic for
each agent with inconsistent timeout handling.

## Deliverables

### AgentAdapter struct
name, description, agent_cli, version_command, input_method (InputMethod enum),
invoke_template, environment (HashMap), token_extraction (TokenExtraction),
provider, model

### Dispatcher struct
- adapters: HashMap<String, AgentAdapter>
- load_adapters(dir: &Path, built_ins: &[AgentAdapter]) → loads YAML files,
  built-in adapters are overridden by user files with same name

### dispatch() method
Input: prompt (BuiltPrompt), adapter (AgentAdapter), workspace (Path)
Output: Result<ExecutionResult>

Algorithm:
1. Write prompt to temp file ({prompt_file})
2. Render invoke template with all variables
3. Set environment variables from adapter config
4. Start process via Command::new("bash").arg("-c").arg(rendered)
5. Wait with timeout (kill if exceeded)
6. Capture stdout, stderr, exit code
7. Clean up temp file
8. Return ExecutionResult

### ExecutionResult struct
exit_code (i32), stdout (String), stderr (String), elapsed (Duration), pid (u32)

### Timeout enforcement
- If elapsed > adapter.timeout: kill process (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after 5s)
- Set exit_code to 124 (same as timeout(1) convention)

### Built-in adapter: Claude Code (Sonnet)
- YAML embedded in binary
- Template: cd {workspace} && claude --print --model claude-sonnet-4-6
  --max-turns 30 --output-format json --verbose < {prompt_file}
- Token extraction: json_field (result.usage.input_tokens/output_tokens)

### Built-in adapter: Generic template
- For users to copy and customize

## Acceptance Criteria
- YAML adapter loads successfully with all fields
- Template rendering substitutes all variables
- Agent process starts and exit code is captured correctly
- Timeout kills process and returns exit 124
- Stdin input method works (redirect from prompt file)
- Missing agent binary → exit 127 (AgentNotFound)
- Environment variables are set for the subprocess
- Temp file is cleaned up even on error/timeout

## Considerations
- Use std::process::Command with .stdout(Stdio::piped()).stderr(Stdio::piped())
- For timeout, use std::thread::spawn + recv_timeout, or tokio::time::timeout
- The prompt file should be in a temp directory, not the workspace (avoid
  polluting the workspace with needle artifacts)
- Phase 2 adds additional built-in adapters (Opus, OpenCode, Codex, Aider)
close_reason: Implemented full agent adapter system with YAML loading, template rendering, process execution with timeout enforcement, and built-in Claude Code adapter. 20 tests cover all acceptance criteria.

title: P3: Hot-reload protocol and rollback
description:
## Task
Workers detect new :stable binary between beads and re-exec seamlessly.
Rollback restores previous :stable.

## Deliverables
- Between LOGGING and SELECTING: compare binary hash to :stable hash
- If different: emit upgrade.detected, complete current cycle, re-exec
  with --resume flag (inherit worker identity)
- --resume: pick up state from heartbeat file + registry, continue from SELECTING
- needle rollback: restore needle-stable.prev → needle-stable
- Workers hot-reload on next cycle after rollback

## Acceptance Criteria
- New :stable detected within one bead cycle
- Re-exec preserves worker ID, tmux session, bead count
- No bead in-flight during re-exec (complete current cycle first)
- Rollback restores previous binary, fleet picks it up

title: P1: Project scaffolding and Cargo workspace
description:
## Task
Initialize the Rust project with Cargo workspace structure, CI configuration,
and module skeleton.

## Background
NEEDLE v2 is a single Rust binary. The module structure must enforce the
dependency graph defined in the architecture: cli → worker → strand/claim/
prompt/dispatch/outcome → bead_store/telemetry/health, with config and types
as leaf dependencies. Getting this right at the start prevents circular
dependencies later.

## Deliverables
- Cargo.toml with workspace configuration
- Module directories: cli/, worker/, strand/, claim/, prompt/, dispatch/,
  outcome/, telemetry/, health/, config/, bead_store/, types/
- Each module has mod.rs with pub interface stubs
- GitHub Actions CI: cargo build, cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo fmt --check
- Cross-compilation targets: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, aarch64-apple-darwin
- .gitignore, LICENSE (MIT), CLAUDE.md with project conventions
- Rust edition 2021, MSRV documented

## Acceptance Criteria
- cargo build succeeds with zero warnings
- cargo test runs (empty test suite passes)
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- CI runs on push to main
- Module dependency graph matches architecture plan (no circular deps)

## Considerations
- Use cargo workspace if we anticipate splitting into library + binary crates,
  but start with a single crate for simplicity. Can split later if needed.
- Pin Rust toolchain version in rust-toolchain.toml for reproducibility.
- Include deny.toml for cargo-deny if desired for dependency auditing.

title: E2E: Failure counter persists across claim cycles — forced mitosis triggers
description:
## What

Prove that when a bead fails verification repeatedly, the failure count is tracked across claim/release cycles and forced mitosis triggers at the configured threshold.

This is the exact bug that caused the bash NEEDLE GLM workers to loop forever: the failure counter reset to 1 on every claim cycle, so force_failure_threshold: 3 never triggered.

## Test Plan

1. Create a workspace with 1 bead that has 3 subtasks in its body
2. Create a test agent adapter that always exits with code 1 (simulates failure)
3. Configure: `mitosis.force_on_failure: true`, `mitosis.force_failure_threshold: 3`
4. Launch a single worker
5. Assert the following sequence:
   - Attempt 1: bead claimed, agent fails (exit 1), failure_count=1, released
   - Attempt 2: same bead re-claimed, agent fails, failure_count=2, released
   - Attempt 3: same bead re-claimed, agent fails, failure_count=3, **forced mitosis triggers**
   - Mitosis creates child beads from the 3 subtasks
   - Parent bead is now blocked by children
   - Worker claims a child bead next (not the parent again)
6. Verify via telemetry:
   - `bead.failure_count` increments correctly (1, 2, 3)
   - `mitosis.forced` event emitted on attempt 3
   - `mitosis.children_created` shows the child bead IDs
   - No infinite loop — worker eventually reaches new work or EXHAUSTED

## Why

This was the #1 operational bug in bash NEEDLE. The Rust version MUST get this right.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failure counter survives claim/release cycles (verified via telemetry)
- Forced mitosis triggers at exactly threshold=3
- Worker does not re-claim the parent after mitosis
- Test runs deterministically in under 30 seconds
close_reason: E2E failure counter persistence test implemented — proves failure counters survive claim/release cycles and forced mitosis triggers at threshold=3

title: P1: Configuration system
description:
## Task
Implement hierarchical configuration loading with validation. Phase 1 supports
global config file and CLI argument overrides. Env vars and workspace config
are Phase 2.

## Background
v1 had no structured configuration — settings were scattered across env vars,
hardcoded values, and YAML files with no validation. Workers would start with
invalid config and fail silently. v2 validates config at boot time and enters
ERRORED state immediately if invalid.

## Deliverables

### Config struct
All configuration fields with defaults:
- agent: { default: String, timeout: u64, adapters_dir: PathBuf }
- worker: { max_workers: u32, launch_stagger_seconds: u64, idle_timeout: u64,
  idle_action: IdleAction, max_claim_retries: u32, identifier_scheme: IdentifierScheme,
  cpu_load_warn: f64, memory_free_warn_mb: u64 }
- workspace: { default: PathBuf, home: PathBuf }
- strands: { pluck: PluckConfig, mend: MendConfig, knot: KnotConfig, ... }
- telemetry: { file_sink: FileSinkConfig }

### ConfigLoader
- load_global(path: &Path) -> Result<Config>
- merge_cli_args(config: &mut Config, args: &CliArgs)
- validate(config: &Config) -> Result<(), Vec<ConfigError>>

### Validation rules
Required: agent.default references valid adapter, workspace has .beads/
Warnings: max_workers > CPU count, heartbeat_ttl < 3 * heartbeat_interval

### Config dump
- Resolved config (all sources merged) as YAML
- Show-source: annotate each value with where it came from

## Acceptance Criteria
- Config loads from YAML file via serde
- Missing file → use defaults (not error)
- Invalid field types → clear error message with field path
- CLI args override file values
- validate() catches all required-field violations
- Default config is valid (passes validation with minimal setup)
- Config struct is Clone (cached at boot, immutable during session)

## Dependencies
- types module (IdleAction, IdentifierScheme enums)

## Considerations
- Use serde with #[serde(default)] for optional fields. This ensures
  partial configs work — users only specify what they want to change.
- Config is loaded once at boot and never reloaded (learned from v1:
  hot-reload amplified bad changes across the fleet).
- Phase 2 will add workspace config (.needle.yaml) and env var overrides.
  Design the merge logic now but only implement global + CLI for Phase 1.
close_reason: Implemented hierarchical config system with nested structs, YAML loading, CLI overrides, validation, and updated all downstream modules

title: P1: PromptBuilder with deterministic prompt construction
description:
## Task
Build deterministic prompts from bead context. Same bead state always produces
the same prompt. Includes bead ID and br close instruction for agent-owned closure.

## Background
v1 prompt construction was ad-hoc — different code paths produced different
prompts for the same bead, and context files were sometimes missing. v2 makes
prompt construction a pure function: bead + workspace config → prompt string.
The prompt hash is logged in telemetry for reproducibility.

## Deliverables

### PromptBuilder struct
- default_template: String (the built-in pluck template)
- context_file_paths: Vec<PathBuf> (from config)
- workspace_instructions: Option<String> (from config)

### build() method
Input: bead (Bead), workspace (Path), config (PromptConfig)
Output: Result<BuiltPrompt>

### BuiltPrompt struct
- content: String (the rendered prompt)
- hash: String (sha256 of content, for telemetry)
- token_estimate: u64 (rough estimate: chars / 4)

### Template rendering
Variables: {bead_id}, {bead_title}, {bead_body}, {workspace_path},
{context_file_contents}, {workspace_instructions}, {worker_id}

### Context file loading
- Read each configured context file from workspace
- If file missing: silently omit (not error)
- Concatenate contents with file path headers

### Default pluck template
(As specified in plan — task, description, workspace, context, instructions,
close command, bead ID)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Same bead + same config → identical prompt string (determinism)
- Prompt hash is stable (same inputs → same hash)
- Missing context files don't cause errors
- Bead ID and br close instruction are always present
- Token estimate is reasonable approximation
- Template variables are all substituted (no literal {bead_id} in output)

## Considerations
- Phase 3 adds configurable templates per strand (mitosis, weave, unravel,
  pulse). Design the template engine now with named templates, but only
  implement the pluck template in Phase 1.
- Use a simple string replacement for variables (not a full template engine
  like Tera). The variables are known at compile time.
close_reason: PromptBuilder with deterministic prompt construction fully implemented with comprehensive tests

title: Phase 3: Advanced Strands & Operations
description:
## Phase Overview
NEEDLE can create work (not just process it), monitor codebase health, and
integrate with external systems. Self-modification via release channels.
Full operational tooling.

## Why This Phase Last
Strands 4-6 (Weave, Unravel, Pulse) create new beads — they add work to
the queue rather than processing it. This is the highest-risk functionality
(v1's weave strand created infinite work loops, mitosis exploded). These
strands are opt-in and heavily guardrailed.

Release channels and hot-reload enable NEEDLE to evolve itself safely —
but only after the core system is proven stable (Phases 1-2).

## Scope
- ~10 additional source files, ~4,000 additional LOC
- Strand 4 (Weave): Gap analysis, bead creation from docs
- Strand 5 (Unravel): Alternatives for HUMAN-blocked beads
- Strand 6 (Pulse): Codebase health scans
- Configurable prompt templates (per-strand, per-workspace)
- Validation gates (pre-closure checks)
- Hook sink (telemetry → webhooks)
- Release channels (testing → stable → fleet hot-reload)
- Canary testing pipeline with defined inputs/outputs
- Hot-reload protocol, rollback mechanism
- Stdout sink, telemetry query commands
- Self-update, doctor command, installer
close_reason: Phase 3 complete: All strands (Weave, Unravel, Pulse), validation gates, hook sink, release channels, canary testing, hot-reload, installer, and integration tests implemented. 603 tests passing.

title: P1: Claimer with workspace flock
description:
## Task
Implement atomic bead claiming with per-workspace flock serialization and
retry logic. The Claimer wraps BeadStore.claim() with the coordination
layer that prevents thundering herd.

## Background
v1 had all workers racing to claim the same bead simultaneously, wasting
O(N²) claim attempts. The flock serializes claims within a workspace so
workers take turns. v1 also attempted to solve this with _needle_acquire_claim_lock
and _needle_release_claim_lock functions that were never implemented (the
bug that broke all workers on 2026-03-20).

## Deliverables

### Claimer struct
- bead_store: Box<dyn BeadStore>
- lock_dir: PathBuf (default: /tmp)
- max_retries: u32 (default: 5)
- retry_backoff_ms: u64 (default: 100)

### claim_next() method
Input: candidates (sorted), actor (worker ID), exclusions (HashSet<BeadId>)
Output: ClaimOutcome (Claimed, AllRaceLost, NoCandidates, StoreError)

Algorithm:
1. Filter candidates by exclusion set
2. For each candidate (in priority order):
   a. Acquire flock on /tmp/needle-claim-{workspace_hash}.lock
   b. Verify bead still claimable (br show --json, check status + assignee)
   c. Attempt br update --claim --actor
   d. Release flock
   e. If success → return Claimed
   f. If race lost → add to exclusions, try next
   g. If max retries → return AllRaceLost
3. No candidates left → NoCandidates

### Flock implementation
- Lock file path: /tmp/needle-claim-{hash}.lock where hash = sha256(workspace_path)[0:16]
- Acquire: flock with 10 second timeout (use fs2 crate or nix::flock)
- Scope: held only for verify + claim (milliseconds, never bead execution)
- On timeout: skip this workspace (return StoreError), do not block

## Acceptance Criteria
- Single worker claims successfully (happy path)
- Two claim attempts on same bead: exactly one succeeds, other gets RaceLost
- Exclusion set prevents re-claiming the same bead
- Flock timeout returns error (not hang)
- Lock file is cleaned up (flock auto-releases on drop)
- claim_next with empty candidates returns NoCandidates
- All errors propagate (no silent swallowing)

## Considerations
- Use the fs2 crate for cross-platform file locking (works on Linux + macOS).
- The workspace_hash must be deterministic — all workers compute the same
  hash for the same workspace path. Use a simple hash, not a crypto hash.
- Phase 2 will use this same flock for mitosis serialization.
close_reason: Implemented Claimer with workspace flock serialization, claim_next with fs2 locking, exclusion filtering, retry/backoff, and 10 unit tests.

title: Phase 1: Core State Machine
description:
## Phase Overview
Build a single binary that processes beads from one workspace using one agent.
The state machine is complete. Telemetry is complete. The tool works end-to-end.
This phase alone is a complete, working system.

## Why This Phase First
The state machine is the skeleton. Every subsequent feature (multi-worker,
strands 2-6, mitosis, self-modification) is an extension of this core loop.
Getting the types, transitions, and error handling right here prevents the
class of bugs that plagued v1 (undefined functions, silent failures, implicit
state). The Rust compiler enforces exhaustive matching on all enums defined
in this phase.

## Scope
- ~15 source files, ~3,000 LOC (Rust)
- Full worker loop: BOOTING → SELECTING → CLAIMING → BUILDING → DISPATCHING → EXECUTING → HANDLING → LOGGING
- Terminal states: EXHAUSTED, STOPPED, ERRORED
- Strands 1 (Pluck) and 7 (Knot) only
- Single workspace, single agent, single worker
- Complete telemetry (file sink, all events)
- CLI: needle run, stop, list, version
- tmux session management

## Success Criteria
- needle run --workspace /path --agent claude-anthropic-sonnet launches in tmux
- Worker claims, dispatches, handles all 6 outcome paths
- Telemetry JSONL has events for every state transition
- needle list/stop work correctly
- Worker loops on multiple beads and exhausts gracefully
- Compiles for Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64

## What Is NOT In Phase 1
- Multi-worker coordination (flock present but single worker)
- Strands 2-6, heartbeat, peer monitoring
- Workspace config (.needle.yaml), multiple adapters
- Cost tracking, budget enforcement
- needle attach, status, config commands
close_reason: All Phase 1 beads complete: core types, config, telemetry, bead_store, claimer, prompt, dispatcher, outcome, pluck, knot, worker state machine, CLI, and integration tests

title: P1: Worker state machine — the core loop
description:
## Task
Implement the worker state machine that ties all Phase 1 components together.
This is the main loop: BOOTING → SELECTING → CLAIMING → BUILDING →
DISPATCHING → EXECUTING → HANDLING → LOGGING → (loop).

## Background
The worker is the central coordinator within a single process. It owns the
state machine and calls into each component module. The state transitions
are explicit — there is no implicit fallthrough and no state that does not
have a defined handler. The worker emits telemetry for every transition.

## Deliverables

### Worker struct
- config: Config
- bead_store: Box<dyn BeadStore>
- claimer: Claimer
- prompt_builder: PromptBuilder
- dispatcher: Dispatcher
- telemetry: TelemetryEmitter
- strands: Vec<Box<dyn Strand>>
- state: State
- current_bead: Option<Bead>
- exclusion_set: HashSet<BeadId>
- retry_count: u32
- beads_processed: u64

### run() method — the main loop
```rust
fn run(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
    self.boot()?;
    loop {
        match self.state {
            State::SELECTING => self.select(),
            State::CLAIMING => self.claim(),
            State::RETRYING => self.retry(),
            State::BUILDING => self.build(),
            State::DISPATCHING => self.dispatch(),
            State::EXECUTING => self.execute(),
            State::HANDLING => self.handle(),
            State::LOGGING => self.log(),
            State::EXHAUSTED => self.exhaust(),
            State::STOPPED => break Ok(()),
            State::ERRORED => break Err(self.error.take().unwrap()),
            // No _ arm — every state is handled
        }
    }
}
```

### State transition methods
Each method:
1. Performs its actions (documented in state-machine plan section)
2. Emits telemetry for the transition
3. Sets self.state to the next state

### Signal handling
- SIGTERM → set state to STOPPED (release claimed bead, clean shutdown)
- SIGINT → same as SIGTERM
- Use signal_hook crate for portable signal handling

### Invariant enforcement
- Assert: current_bead.is_some() only during BUILDING through HANDLING
- Assert: exclusion_set is cleared on every transition to SELECTING
- Assert: retry_count resets on transition to SELECTING

## Acceptance Criteria
- Full loop executes: boot → select → claim → build → dispatch → execute →
  handle → log → select (next bead)
- All 6 outcome paths reach LOGGING and loop back to SELECTING
- SIGTERM during EXECUTING → kill agent → HANDLING(interrupted) → STOPPED
- SIGTERM during SELECTING → STOPPED (no bead to release)
- Config invalid at boot → ERRORED
- Bead store unreachable at boot → ERRORED (after retry with backoff)
- Telemetry event emitted for EVERY state transition (verify in JSONL)
- Worker exits cleanly from STOPPED and ERRORED

## Dependencies
- All Phase 1 component beads: types, config, bead_store, telemetry,
  claimer, prompt_builder, dispatcher, outcome_handler, pluck, knot

## Considerations
- The main loop is synchronous in Phase 1 (single worker). Phase 2 adds
  heartbeat emission from a background thread, which requires the worker
  to be partially async or use a separate thread for heartbeats.
- Consider using an enum-dispatch pattern for state handlers to keep the
  match arms thin and push logic into the component modules.
close_reason: Worker state machine with explicit state tracking, signal handling, boot validation, 10 unit tests

title: P2: Database auto-recovery from JSONL
description:
## Task
Detect SQLite corruption in br operations and auto-repair from JSONL.

## Background
beads_rust has a known corruption bug (FrankenSQLite, upstream #171). JSONL
is the authoritative data source — it is append-only and immune to SQLite
corruption. Recovery always rebuilds from JSONL.

## Deliverables
- Detection: catch "database disk image is malformed" from br commands
- Recovery: br doctor --repair → if fail: rm .beads/beads.db + br sync --import
- Proactive: Mend strand runs br doctor periodically (every db_check_interval beads)
- Telemetry: health.db_repaired, health.db_rebuilt events

## Acceptance Criteria
- Corrupted db detected on br command failure
- Auto-repair restores functionality without human intervention
- Full rebuild (rm + sync) works when repair fails
- Persistent corruption (JSONL itself bad) → ERRORED state
close_reason: Implemented database auto-recovery from JSONL: corruption detection, recovery pipeline (doctor → repair → full rebuild), BeadStore trait extensions, telemetry events, 8 new tests

title: P1: Strand 7 (Knot) — exhaustion alerting with three-state verification
description:
## Task
Implement the Knot strand: all work-finding strategies exhausted, verify
the reason, alert human if appropriate.

## Background
v1 starvation alerts had a 100% false positive rate across 16 alerts because
the system used the same broken code path for verification. Knot implements
a three-state model: the reasons "no beads exist," "all beads claimed," and
"beads invisible" are three different conditions requiring different responses.
Verification uses a DIFFERENT code path from the one that reported no work.

## Deliverables

### Strand trait implementation
```rust
impl Strand for KnotStrand {
    fn name(&self) -> &str { "knot" }
    fn enabled(&self, config: &Config) -> bool { true } // always on
    fn evaluate(&self, context: &WorkerContext) -> Result<StrandResult>
}
```

### Three-state verification
1. Query bead store for ALL beads (not just ready): show total count
2. Query for beads with status=open: count
3. Query for beads with status=in_progress: count + who claims them
4. Diagnose:
   a. Total = 0 → NO_BEADS_EXIST (queue genuinely empty, normal idle)
   b. All in_progress → ALL_CLAIMED (other workers busy, wait)
   c. Open beads exist but Pluck found none → INVISIBLE (config error, alert)

### Alert bead creation
- Only create alert if diagnosis is INVISIBLE
- Rate limit: max 1 alert per workspace per config.knot.alert_cooldown_minutes
- Include diagnostics: total beads, open count, in_progress count, claimed_by
  list, exclude_labels config, workspace path
- Label alert bead as "starvation-alert"

### Exhaustion handling
- First exhaustion cycle: emit worker.idle telemetry
- Repeated (>config.knot.exhaustion_threshold): create alert if warranted
- Always return StrandResult::NoWork (Knot never produces work)

## Acceptance Criteria
- NO_BEADS_EXIST → no alert, just telemetry
- ALL_CLAIMED → no alert, just telemetry
- INVISIBLE → alert bead created with diagnostics
- Alert rate limiting: second call within cooldown creates no new alert
- Verification query is DIFFERENT from Pluck query (different code path)
- Telemetry includes diagnosis reason

## Considerations
- The verification query must bypass Pluck's filters to get a true picture
  of the queue. If Pluck excluded a bead due to a label and there are no
  other beads, Knot should detect this as "beads exist but are filtered"
  (which is normal, not an alert — unless ALL beads are filtered, which
  may indicate a config issue).
close_reason: Implemented with three-state exhaustion diagnosis (NoBeadsExist, AllClaimed, Invisible) and alert rate limiting

title: P1: BeadStore trait and br CLI implementation
description:
## Task
Define the abstract BeadStore trait and implement the primary backend that
wraps the br CLI with JSON parsing.

## Background
v1 called br directly throughout the codebase with inconsistent error handling.
JSON parsing failures from br --json were silently treated as empty results,
causing the starvation false positive epidemic (100% false positive rate across
16 alerts). v2 abstracts bead operations behind a trait where all methods
return Result and JSON parsing failures are explicit errors.

## Deliverables

### BeadStore trait
```rust
trait BeadStore: Send + Sync {
    fn ready(&self, workspace: &Path, filters: &Filters) -> Result<Vec<Bead>>;
    fn show(&self, id: &BeadId) -> Result<Bead>;
    fn claim(&self, id: &BeadId, actor: &str) -> Result<ClaimResult>;
    fn release(&self, id: &BeadId) -> Result<()>;
    fn labels(&self, id: &BeadId) -> Result<Vec<String>>;
    fn add_label(&self, id: &BeadId, label: &str) -> Result<()>;
    fn doctor_repair(&self) -> Result<RepairReport>;
}
```

### BrCliStore implementation
- Shells out to br with --json flag
- Parses JSON output via serde_json
- Maps br exit codes to ClaimResult variants:
  - Exit 0 → Claimed(bead)
  - Exit 4 → RaceLost { claimed_by }
  - Other → NotClaimable { reason }
- JSON parse failure → explicit error (NOT empty Vec)
- br not found → explicit error (NOT silent skip)
- Workspace context: all br operations cd to workspace first

### Filters struct
- status: Option<Vec<String>>
- assignee: Option<String> (None = unassigned)
- exclude_labels: Vec<String>

## Acceptance Criteria
- All trait methods return Result (no panics, no unwrap)
- JSON parsing of br ready --json output tested with real br output samples
- Malformed JSON → error with context (which command, what output)
- br not on PATH → clear error at construction time
- Labels read via br label list (NOT br show --json, which omits labels — v1 bug)
- Unit tests with captured br output (but integration tests use real br)

## Considerations
- The trait is Send + Sync because it will be called from async worker tasks.
- Use std::process::Command for br invocation. Capture both stdout and stderr.
- Consider a BrCliStore::new(br_path: PathBuf) constructor that validates
  the binary exists and is executable.
- The ready() method must handle the case where br returns valid JSON but
  with zero results (empty array) — this is NOT an error, it means the queue
  is empty.
close_reason: Implemented BeadStore trait with Filters/RepairReport, BrCliStore with real br CLI invocations, JSON parse error handling, claim race detection, and 6 unit tests. Updated claim and outcome modules to use new API.

title: Genesis: NEEDLE v2 Implementation
description:
## Genesis Bead
Tied to plan: /home/coding/NEEDLE/plan/plan.md

## Overview
NEEDLE v2 is a complete rewrite of the NEEDLE bead processing orchestrator.
The v1 implementation (45K lines of bash) is retired in NEEDLE-deprecated.
v2 is a compiled Rust binary implementing a deterministic state machine
for processing beads with explicit outcome paths.

## Design Principles
1. Deterministic order — same queue state → same bead ordering
2. Explicit outcome paths — every outcome has a handler, enforced by type system
3. Platform and model agnostic — wraps any headless CLI
4. Observable by default — structured telemetry for every state transition
5. Self-healing — automatic recovery from crashes, stale claims, corruption
6. Separation of concerns — orchestrator, agent, bead store each have one job

## Progress
- [ ] Phase 1: Core State Machine (~3,000 LOC)
- [ ] Phase 2: Multi-Worker Fleet (~4,000 LOC)
- [ ] Phase 3: Advanced Strands & Operations (~4,000 LOC)

## Key Decisions
- Language: Rust (exhaustive matching, Result<T,E>, serde_json, single binary)
- Bead store: Abstract trait over br CLI (not direct SQLite)
- Claim atomicity: br update --claim + per-workspace flock
- Agent closure: Agent closes beads, not NEEDLE
- No built-in mitosis explosion: child-aware dedup prevents duplicates
- Self-modification: Allowed via release channels with canary testing
- Fleet sizing: Runtime-bounded (CPU, RAM, provider throughput), not arbitrary limits
close_reason: NEEDLE v2 implementation complete. All three phases delivered: Phase 1 (core state machine), Phase 2 (multi-worker fleet), Phase 3 (advanced strands and operations). 603 tests passing across unit, integration, and real-br test suites.

title: Enforce dedicated tmux sessions for all needle worker launches
description:
## Problem

When \`needle run\` is invoked from inside an existing tmux session (\`$TMUX\` is set), it bypasses \`launch_workers()\` and runs the worker directly in the current process. This means \`--count\` is ignored, no dedicated tmux sessions are created, and the worker shares its session with whatever else the user is doing.

## Desired Behavior

\`needle run\` should **always** create dedicated tmux sessions for workers, regardless of whether it is invoked from inside tmux. If \`$TMUX\` is set, needle should still spawn new tmux sessions (using \`tmux new-session -d\`) rather than running inline.

The only exception is the re-entrant case: when needle is re-invoked by tmux inside a session it just created (detectable via \`--identifier\` + \`--count 1\` matching the inner invocation pattern from \`launch_in_tmux()\`). This existing self-invocation path should continue to run the worker directly.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] \`needle run --count N\` from inside tmux creates N separate \`needle-{agent}-{name}\` sessions
- [ ] The direct-mode path is only reachable via the inner re-invocation from \`launch_in_tmux()\`
- [ ] \`--count 1\` from inside tmux still creates a dedicated session (not inline)
- [ ] Add an internal flag or env var (e.g. \`NEEDLE_INNER=1\`) to distinguish re-entrant calls from user invocations
- [ ] Unit test for the detection logic

title: P1: Telemetry system with file sink
description:
## Task
Implement the structured telemetry system with JSONL file sink. Every state
transition produces a typed event. The system is non-blocking and separate
from agent output.

## Background
v1 used _needle_debug() which wrote to stdout, corrupting JSON in subshells.
This was the root cause of the starvation false positive epidemic — debug
output mixed with br --json output, producing malformed JSON that was silently
treated as empty results. v2 telemetry is a completely separate system that
never touches stdout/stderr.

## Deliverables

### TelemetryEvent struct
timestamp (DateTime<Utc>), event_type (String), worker_id (String),
session_id (String), sequence (u64, monotonic), bead_id (Option<BeadId>),
workspace (Option<PathBuf>), data (serde_json::Value), duration_ms (Option<u64>)

### TelemetryEmitter
- emit(event: TelemetryEvent) — non-blocking, queues event
- Background writer thread/task that drains queue to sinks
- Buffer in memory if sink is slow; drop after configurable threshold
- Never blocks the worker loop

### FileSink
- Writes JSONL to ~/.needle/logs/<worker-id>-<session-id>.jsonl
- One line per event, append-only
- Atomic writes (write to buffer, flush periodically)
- Rotation: new file per session (Phase 1), size-based rotation (Phase 2)

### Event factory functions
Convenience constructors for each event type:
- worker_started(agent, model, config_hash, version) → TelemetryEvent
- worker_stopped(beads_processed, uptime_seconds, reason) → TelemetryEvent
- bead_claim_attempted(bead_id, retry_number) → TelemetryEvent
- agent_dispatched(agent_name, model, pid, prompt_hash) → TelemetryEvent
- outcome_classified(outcome, exit_code) → TelemetryEvent
- ... (all 25+ events in the catalog)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Events are valid JSON (parse back with serde_json)
- Sequence numbers are monotonically increasing within a session
- Timestamps are UTC ISO 8601 with millisecond precision
- File sink creates log directory if missing
- Emitting an event when sink is broken does NOT panic or block
- Complete session is replayable from JSONL (every state transition has an event)
- No telemetry output goes to stdout or stderr

## Considerations
- Use crossbeam-channel or tokio::mpsc for the event queue.
- The session_id should be a short random string (e.g., 8 hex chars) generated
  at worker boot. This disambiguates log files from different sessions.
- Phase 2 adds stdout sink and hook sink. Design the sink trait now:
  trait TelemetrySink { fn write(&self, event: &TelemetryEvent) -> Result<()>; }
close_reason: Implemented structured telemetry system with JSONL file sink, non-blocking emitter, 25 event kinds, TelemetrySink trait, FileSink with BufWriter, 37 tests passing

title: P1: Strand 1 (Pluck) — primary bead selection
description:
## Task
Implement the Pluck strand: query, filter, and sort beads from the assigned
workspace in deterministic priority order.

## Background
Pluck handles >90% of all bead processing. It is the only strand that
directly produces work for the agent. Its ordering guarantee is critical:
given the same queue state, every worker computes the same candidate list.
This makes the system predictable and debuggable — you can look at the
queue and know exactly which bead will be claimed next.

## Deliverables

### Strand trait implementation
```rust
impl Strand for PluckStrand {
    fn name(&self) -> &str { "pluck" }
    fn enabled(&self, config: &Config) -> bool { true } // always on
    fn evaluate(&self, context: &WorkerContext) -> Result<StrandResult>
}
```

### evaluate() algorithm
1. Query bead store: ready(workspace, filters) where filters = {
     status: open, assignee: None (unassigned),
     exclude_labels: config.strands.pluck.exclude_labels
   }
2. Filter: remove beads in context.exclusion_set
3. Sort: by (priority ASC, created_at ASC) — deterministic
4. If candidates.is_empty() → return StrandResult::NoWork
5. Return StrandResult::BeadFound(candidates)

### StrandRunner
Evaluates strands in sequence:
```rust
fn run_strands(strands: &[Box<dyn Strand>], ctx: &WorkerContext) -> StrandWaterfallResult {
    for strand in strands {
        if !strand.enabled(&ctx.config) { continue; }
        match strand.evaluate(ctx)? {
            BeadFound(candidates) => return Ok(WaterfallResult::Candidates(candidates)),
            WorkCreated => return Ok(WaterfallResult::RestartFromTop),
            NoWork => continue,
            Error(e) => { emit_telemetry(e); continue; }
        }
    }
    Ok(WaterfallResult::Exhausted)
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Candidates sorted by (priority, created_at) — verified with multi-priority queue
- Beads with excluded labels are filtered out
- Exclusion set beads are filtered out
- Empty queue returns NoWork
- Bead store error returns Error (not NoWork — different semantics)
- Determinism: same queue state → same ordering (property test)
- Telemetry: strand.evaluated event emitted with result and duration

## Considerations
- The sort must be stable and produce identical results across platforms.
  Use a stable sort with a total ordering key. If two beads have identical
  (priority, created_at), break ties by bead ID (lexicographic).
- The exclude_labels default is [deferred, human, blocked] but configurable.
close_reason: PluckStrand with deterministic (priority, created_at, id) ordering, label filtering, store error handling, and 13 unit tests.

title: P1: CLI commands and tmux session management
description:
## Task
Implement the CLI parser (needle run/stop/list/version) with --help support
and tmux session management for worker processes.

## Background
NEEDLE self-invokes into tmux: needle run creates a tmux session and re-execs
itself inside it. This provides persistent sessions that survive SSH disconnects
and allows attach/detach for monitoring. Session naming follows the pattern
needle-{agent}-{provider}-{model}-{identifier} for discoverability.

## Deliverables

### CLI parser (clap)
- needle run [--workspace PATH] [--agent NAME] [--count N] [--identifier NAME]
  [--timeout SECONDS] [--resume]
- needle stop [--all | --identifier NAME]
- needle list [--format table|json]
- needle version
- All commands support --help / -h with descriptions and defaults

### tmux integration
- needle run (outside tmux): create tmux session, exec self inside it
- needle run (inside tmux): start worker directly
- Session naming: needle-{agent}-{provider}-{model}-{identifier}
- Default identifiers: NATO alphabet (alpha, bravo, charlie, ...)
- needle stop: send SIGTERM to worker process in tmux session
- needle stop --all: iterate all needle-* tmux sessions
- needle list: tmux list-sessions filtered to needle-* prefix

### Version output
needle version → "needle 0.1.0 (rust, linux x86_64)"
Include: version, language, platform, architecture

### Help system
- Top-level: needle --help shows all subcommands with one-line descriptions
- Per-command: needle run --help shows all flags with descriptions and defaults
- Generated from clap derive macros (help text = source of truth)

## Acceptance Criteria
- needle run outside tmux creates session and re-execs
- needle run inside tmux starts worker directly (no recursive tmux)
- needle stop sends SIGTERM and session exits cleanly
- needle list shows running sessions with format table or json
- needle version outputs correct info
- --help for every command shows complete flag documentation
- Invalid flags produce helpful error messages (clap default behavior)
- NATO alphabet identifiers: 1st worker=alpha, 2nd=bravo, etc.

## Dependencies
- Worker state machine (needle run invokes Worker::run())
- Config system (default values shown in --help)

## Considerations
- Use clap v4 with derive macros for the CLI parser. This generates
  --help automatically from doc comments and field attributes.
- The tmux detection (am I inside tmux?) checks $TMUX env var.
- Phase 1 only launches 1 worker (--count > 1 is Phase 2).
  Validate --count=1 in Phase 1 and error on >1.
- Phase 2 adds: needle attach, needle status, needle config, needle doctor
close_reason: Implemented full Phase 1 CLI: clap v4 derive parser with run/stop/list/version subcommands, tmux session management, NATO alphabet identifiers, Phase 1 count validation. 164 tests passing.

title: P2: Heartbeat system with TTL-based stale detection
description:
## Task
Implement file-based heartbeat emission from a dedicated background thread.
Other workers read heartbeat files to detect crashed or stuck peers.

## Background
v1 had no heartbeat. When a worker crashed, its claimed bead was stuck
in_progress forever until a human noticed. The heartbeat system enables
automatic detection: if a worker stops updating its heartbeat file, peers
know it is dead or stuck.

## Deliverables

### Heartbeat file
Path: ~/.needle/state/heartbeats/<worker-id>.json
Contents: { worker_id, pid, state, current_bead, workspace, last_heartbeat,
started_at, beads_processed, session }

### Heartbeat emitter
- Background thread, runs independently of main worker loop
- Writes heartbeat every heartbeat_interval (default: 30s)
- Atomic write: write temp file, rename (prevents partial reads)
- Updates state and current_bead from shared worker state (Arc<Mutex>)
- Continues emitting during EXECUTING (proof of liveness)

### Heartbeat reader
- read_all_heartbeats(dir: &Path) → Vec<HeartbeatFile>
- is_stale(heartbeat: &HeartbeatFile, ttl: Duration) → bool
- check_pid_alive(pid: u32) → bool (kill -0 equivalent)

### Cleanup on shutdown
- Remove own heartbeat file on graceful STOPPED
- Best-effort removal on ERRORED

## Acceptance Criteria
- Heartbeat file appears within heartbeat_interval of worker start
- Heartbeat updates continuously during EXECUTING (not just between beads)
- Stale detection works: stop heartbeat thread, verify is_stale() returns true
- Atomic write: concurrent reader never sees partial JSON
- PID check correctly identifies dead vs alive processes
- Heartbeat file removed on clean shutdown

## Considerations
- Use std::thread::spawn for the background emitter (not tokio — keep simple)
- Share worker state via Arc<Mutex<WorkerHeartbeatState>> between main
  thread and heartbeat thread
- heartbeat_ttl should be >= 3 * heartbeat_interval to tolerate delays
close_reason: Implemented file-based heartbeat system: background std::thread emitter with configurable interval (default 30s), atomic write-rename for safe concurrent reads, shared worker state via Arc<Mutex>, TTL-based stale detection (default 5min), PID liveness check, heartbeat file cleanup on shutdown, HealthConfig with validation. 12 unit tests.

title: E2E: Telemetry event completeness — every state transition emits
description:
## What

Validate that the telemetry contract from plan.md is actually honored: every state transition and every outcome emits a structured telemetry event to the JSONL log.

## Test Plan

Run the single-worker lifecycle E2E test and parse the resulting telemetry log. Assert the COMPLETE event sequence:

```
worker.started          — BOOTING
worker.state_changed    — BOOTING → SELECTING
strand.pluck.started    — SELECTING
strand.pluck.found      — candidate bead
bead.claim.attempted    — CLAIMING
bead.claim.succeeded    — claim won
worker.state_changed    — CLAIMING → BUILDING
prompt.built            — BUILDING (includes bead_id, prompt_hash, token_estimate)
worker.state_changed    — BUILDING → DISPATCHING
agent.dispatched        — DISPATCHING (agent name, workspace, bead_id)
worker.state_changed    — DISPATCHING → EXECUTING
agent.exited            — EXECUTING (exit_code, duration_ms)
worker.state_changed    — EXECUTING → HANDLING
outcome.classified      — HANDLING (outcome type: Success/Failure/Crash)
bead.closed             — agent closed the bead (or bead.released on failure)
worker.state_changed    — HANDLING → LOGGING
worker.state_changed    — LOGGING → SELECTING
strand.pluck.no_work    — SELECTING (no more beads)
strand.knot.exhausted   — KNOT
worker.state_changed    — SELECTING → EXHAUSTED
worker.finished         — terminal
```

## Why

Design Principle 4: "Observable by default. Every state transition, claim attempt, dispatch, and outcome emits structured telemetry. A silent worker is a broken worker."

If any event is missing, downstream monitoring (Fabric, dashboards, the Mend strand's peer detection) will have blind spots.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every event in the sequence above is present in the JSONL log
- Events have correct timestamps (monotonically increasing)
- Events include the required data fields per plan.md spec
- No duplicate events for single transitions
- Missing events cause test failure with clear error message identifying the gap
close_reason: Implemented E2E telemetry completeness test with 80 assertions. Fixed BeadCompleted emission bug and BeadStatus::Closed variant.

title: Agent output streaming and normalized telemetry for FABRIC observability
description:
## Summary

NEEDLE currently captures agent stdout/stderr in full after process exit, uses it only for token extraction, then discards it. FABRIC's dashboard mockups show tool-level activity (Edit, Read, Grep calls) but have no data source to back them — NEEDLE's telemetry treats the agent as a black box.

## Goal

Stream agent stdout during execution through an adapter-specific transform that normalizes output into a universal JSONL format. Write the normalized stream to per-bead log files that FABRIC can tail in real-time.

## Design

### Architecture

```
Agent process stdout
  → tee (capture for token extraction + stream to transform)
  → adapter-specific transform binary (e.g., needle-transform-claude)
  → normalized JSONL written to ~/.needle/logs/<worker>-<bead>.agent.jsonl
  → FABRIC tails these files alongside orchestration telemetry
```

### Adapter YAML extension

```yaml
output_transform: "needle-transform-claude"  # optional, per-adapter
```

When `output_transform` is set, NEEDLE pipes agent stdout through the transform binary during execution. When absent, behavior is unchanged (stdout captured post-exit for token extraction only).

### Normalized event format

```jsonl
{"type":"tool_call","tool":"Edit","path":"src/auth.ts","ts":1711100400}
{"type":"tool_call","tool":"Read","path":"src/types.ts","ts":1711100401}
{"type":"agent_message","content":"I'll update the auth flow...","ts":1711100402}
{"type":"tokens","input":1200,"output":450,"ts":1711100403}
```

### Key constraints

- NEEDLE must remain agent-agnostic — no agent-specific parsing in core
- Transform is optional — adapters without one work exactly as today
- Token extraction must still work (tee stdout, don't replace the capture)
- Agent log files must be rotatable/cleanable by existing log management
- Transform failures must not kill the agent process

## Files

- `src/dispatch/mod.rs` — stdout piping, tee, transform spawn
- `src/config/mod.rs` — new `output_transform` adapter field
- `src/telemetry/mod.rs` — new agent-level event types (optional, for indexing)

title: Add output_transform field to adapter YAML schema
description:
## Deliverable

Extend the adapter YAML schema to support an optional `output_transform` field that specifies a binary/script to normalize agent stdout into universal JSONL.

## Acceptance criteria

- `AdapterConfig` struct in dispatch gains `output_transform: Option<String>` field
- Field is deserialized from adapter YAML files
- Existing adapters without the field continue to work (backward compatible)
- `needle test-agent <name>` validates the transform binary exists on PATH when specified
- Schema documented in adapter YAML comments

## Example

```yaml
# ~/.config/needle/adapters/claude-sonnet.yaml
agent_cli: claude
invoke_template: "cd {workspace} && claude --print --output-format json < {prompt_file}"
output_transform: "needle-transform-claude"
token_extraction:
  format: json
  input_path: "usage.input_tokens"
  output_path: "usage.output_tokens"
```

## Files

- `src/dispatch/mod.rs` — AdapterConfig struct, YAML deserialization
- `src/dispatch/adapter.rs` (if exists) — adapter loading/validation

title: Tee agent stdout during execution for concurrent capture and streaming
description:
## Deliverable

Refactor the dispatch module's agent execution to tee stdout in real-time instead of collecting it post-exit. One branch feeds the existing token extraction buffer; the other feeds the output transform (if configured) or is discarded.

## Acceptance criteria

- Agent stdout is readable line-by-line during execution, not only after exit
- The existing `ExecutionResult.stdout` field is still populated with the full captured output (token extraction must still work)
- stderr capture is unchanged (still collected post-exit)
- No behavioral change when `output_transform` is not configured — stdout is captured exactly as before
- Pipe buffer deadlock prevention is maintained (the current dual-task approach must not regress)
- Agent process is not blocked by slow transform consumers

## Technical notes

Replace the single `read_to_end` on stdout with a `BufReader::lines()` loop that:
1. Appends each line to the capture buffer (for ExecutionResult.stdout)
2. Writes each line to the transform process stdin (when configured)

Use a bounded channel or pipe to decouple the agent from the transform so a slow/stuck transform cannot back-pressure the agent.

## Files

- `src/dispatch/mod.rs:561-591` — current stdout/stderr capture logic

title: Spawn output_transform process and write normalized JSONL to per-bead log file
description:
## Deliverable

When an adapter specifies `output_transform`, spawn the transform binary as a child process during agent execution. Pipe tee'd agent stdout into the transform's stdin. Write the transform's stdout (normalized JSONL) to a per-bead agent log file.

## Acceptance criteria

- Transform binary is spawned with: stdin = piped (receives agent stdout lines), stdout = piped (writes to log file), stderr = inherited (errors visible in worker terminal)
- Output written to `~/.needle/logs/<worker>-<bead_id>.agent.jsonl`
- Transform process is killed when agent process exits (regardless of exit code)
- Transform crash/failure does NOT affect agent execution or outcome classification
- Transform exit code is logged as a telemetry event but does not alter the worker state machine
- File is flushed and closed before the HANDLING state begins
- When no `output_transform` is configured, no transform process is spawned and no agent log file is created

## Error handling

- Transform binary not found on PATH: log warning telemetry event, skip transform, continue normally
- Transform exits early: log warning, stop writing to its stdin, continue agent capture normally
- Transform writes invalid output: not NEEDLE's problem — FABRIC validates on read

## Files

- `src/dispatch/mod.rs` — transform spawn, pipe wiring, cleanup

title: Define normalized agent event schema and document the contract
description:
## Deliverable

Define the universal JSONL schema that all output transforms must produce. This is the contract between adapter-specific transforms and FABRIC.

## Acceptance criteria

- Schema defined as a Rust struct/enum in a new `src/agent_event.rs` module (for validation tooling, not runtime use)
- Schema also documented in `docs/agent-event-schema.md` for transform authors
- Covers at minimum these event types:
  - `tool_call` — agent invoked a tool (tool name, arguments, file path if applicable)
  - `tool_result` — tool returned a result (success/failure, output summary)
  - `agent_message` — agent emitted text (content, role)
  - `tokens` — token usage update (input, output, model)
  - `error` — agent-level error (message, recoverable flag)
- All events have: `type` (string), `ts` (unix epoch float), and event-specific fields
- Schema is versioned (`"schema_version": 1` top-level field)

## Non-goals

- NEEDLE does not validate transform output at runtime — that is FABRIC's concern
- NEEDLE does not parse these events — it writes them opaquely to disk

## Example

```jsonl
{"schema_version":1,"type":"tool_call","tool":"Edit","path":"src/auth.ts","args":{"old_string":"foo","new_string":"bar"},"ts":1711100400.123}
{"schema_version":1,"type":"tool_result","tool":"Edit","success":true,"ts":1711100400.456}
{"schema_version":1,"type":"agent_message","role":"assistant","content":"Updated the auth flow","ts":1711100401.0}
{"schema_version":1,"type":"tokens","input":1200,"output":450,"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","ts":1711100403.0}
```
close_reason: Defined normalized agent event schema v1. Added src/agent_event.rs with AgentEvent envelope and EventPayload enum covering all 5 required event types. Documented the JSONL contract in docs/agent-event-schema.md. 6 tests pass.

title: Implement needle-transform-claude for Claude Code --output-format json
description:
## Deliverable

Build the first reference transform binary that reads Claude Code's \`--output-format json\` stdout and emits the normalized agent event JSONL schema.

## Acceptance criteria

- Standalone binary: \`needle-transform-claude\`
- Reads Claude's JSON output from stdin line-by-line
- Emits normalized JSONL to stdout
- Maps Claude events:
  - \`tool_use\` blocks → \`tool_call\` events (tool name, input args, file path extraction for Read/Edit/Grep/Glob/Write)
  - \`tool_result\` blocks → \`tool_result\` events
  - \`text\` blocks → \`agent_message\` events
  - \`usage\` fields → \`tokens\` events (input_tokens, output_tokens, model)
  - \`error\` events → \`error\` events
- Handles partial/malformed JSON lines gracefully (skip with stderr warning, don't crash)
- Exits cleanly when stdin closes (agent process finished)
- Written in Rust, built alongside NEEDLE (same workspace, separate binary target in Cargo.toml)

## Testing

- Unit tests with captured Claude \`--output-format json\` output samples
- Integration test: pipe a sample Claude session through the transform and validate normalized output

## Files

- \`src/bin/needle_transform_claude.rs\` (or \`src/transforms/claude.rs\` + binary target)
- \`Cargo.toml\` — add binary target
close_reason: Implemented needle-transform-claude binary: reads Claude --output-format json NDJSON from stdin, emits normalized agent event JSONL (schema v1). Maps tool_use→tool_call (with path extraction), tool_result→tool_result, text→agent_message, usage→tokens, errors→error. Handles malformed lines gracefully. 25 unit tests + 4 integration tests pass.

title: Implement needle-transform-codex for OpenAI Codex CLI output
description:
## Deliverable

Build a transform binary that reads OpenAI Codex CLI stdout and emits the normalized agent event JSONL schema.

## Acceptance criteria

- Standalone binary: \`needle-transform-codex\`
- Reads Codex CLI output from stdin line-by-line
- Emits normalized JSONL to stdout matching the agent event schema
- Maps Codex-specific events to universal types (tool_call, tool_result, agent_message, tokens, error)
- Handles partial/malformed lines gracefully
- Exits cleanly when stdin closes

## Notes

- Lower priority than Claude transform — Claude is the primary agent
- May need sample Codex output captured to develop against
- Written in Rust, separate binary target in Cargo.toml
close_reason: Implemented needle-transform-codex binary (926 lines, 27 unit tests). Reads Codex CLI JSONL from stdin, maps Codex ThreadEvent types to normalized agent event schema v1: command_execution→shell, file_change→file_edit, mcp_tool_call→actual tool name, plus agent_message, tokens, error events. Graceful degradation on malformed input, clean exit on stdin close. Builds and passes all tests with zero clippy warnings.

title: Emit telemetry events for transform lifecycle (started, completed, failed)
description:
## Deliverable

Add telemetry events so the transform pipeline itself is observable. FABRIC and \`needle logs\` should be able to show whether transforms are running, succeeding, or failing.

## Acceptance criteria

- New telemetry events:
  - \`transform.started\` — fields: \`bead_id\`, \`transform_binary\`, \`agent\`
  - \`transform.completed\` — fields: \`bead_id\`, \`events_written\`, \`duration_ms\`
  - \`transform.failed\` — fields: \`bead_id\`, \`error\`, \`exit_code\`
  - \`transform.skipped\` — fields: \`bead_id\`, \`reason\` (binary not found, not configured)
- Events added to the \`TelemetryEvent\` enum with exhaustive match arms
- Events emitted at the appropriate points in the dispatch module
- \`needle logs --filter "transform.*"\` can filter to these events

## Files

- \`src/telemetry/mod.rs\` — new event variants, event_type() mapping, to_data() serialization
- \`src/dispatch/mod.rs\` — emit events at transform spawn/completion/failure points

title: Agent log file rotation and cleanup in mend strand
description:
## Deliverable

Agent log files (\`<worker>-<bead>.agent.jsonl\`) will accumulate in \`~/.needle/logs/\`. The mend strand should clean up stale agent logs alongside its existing orchestration log management.

## Acceptance criteria

- Mend strand deletes \`.agent.jsonl\` files older than the configured log retention period
- Retention period uses the existing \`logs.retention_days\` config value (no new config key)
- Agent logs for currently-executing beads are never deleted (check against active claims)
- Cleanup count is included in the existing \`mend.cycle_summary\` telemetry event (new field: \`agent_logs_cleaned\`)
- No cleanup runs when there are no agent log files (avoid unnecessary filesystem scans)

## Files

- \`src/strand/mend.rs\` (or wherever mend logic lives) — add agent log cleanup step
- \`src/telemetry/mod.rs\` — extend \`mend.cycle_summary\` data with \`agent_logs_cleaned\`

title: Update built-in adapter YAMLs to include output_transform for Claude and Codex
description:
## Deliverable

Update the shipped adapter YAML files to reference their corresponding transform binaries. Users who upgrade NEEDLE should get agent-level observability automatically.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`claude-sonnet.yaml\` and \`claude-opus.yaml\` include \`output_transform: needle-transform-claude\`
- \`codex.yaml\` includes \`output_transform: needle-transform-codex\` (when codex transform is ready)

- \`needle doctor\` warns if an adapter references a transform binary that is not on PATH
- Transform binaries are installed to \`~/.needle/bin/\` alongside the needle binary (or to \`~/.local/bin/\`)

## Files
