title: E2E: Multi-worker coordination — no duplicate claims
description:
## What

Prove that multiple needle workers operating on the same workspace never claim the same bead, and all beads are eventually processed exactly once.

## Test Plan

1. Create a workspace with 10 simple beads (each: "create file DONE-{n}")
2. Create a fast test agent adapter (~1s execution per bead)
3. Launch `needle run --count 3` (3 concurrent workers)
4. Wait for all workers to reach EXHAUSTED (max 60s)
5. Assert:
   - All 10 beads are CLOSED
   - Each bead was claimed exactly once (check telemetry for duplicate bead.claimed events)
   - No worker panicked (check exit codes)
   - 10 DONE-{n} files exist
   - Workers used staggered startup (check timestamps)
   - Workspace flock prevented thundering herd (telemetry shows serialized claims)

## Why

The bash NEEDLE had a bug where multiple workers claimed the same bead simultaneously (we observed 4 workers all claiming mta-my-way-s23.1.9). The Rust version must prove this can't happen.

## Acceptance Criteria
- 3 workers, 10 beads, 0 duplicates, 10 closures
- Test is deterministic (no flaky races)
- Runs in under 60 seconds
close_reason: Implemented tests/e2e/multi_worker.sh: 3 concurrent workers, 10 beads, validated zero duplicate claims via telemetry aggregation.

title: Fix: needle run panics — tokio::spawn called outside runtime context
description:
## What

`needle run` panics at src/telemetry/mod.rs:1535 with:

```
thread 'main' panicked at src/telemetry/mod.rs:1535:9:
there is no reactor running, must be called from the context of a Tokio 1.x runtime
```

## Why

`Telemetry::new()` calls `spawn_writer()` which uses `tokio::spawn()`. This requires an active Tokio runtime. The runtime is created at cli/mod.rs:457 via `Runtime::new()` + `block_on()`, but `Telemetry::new()` is called during `Worker::new()` construction which happens BEFORE `rt.block_on(worker.run())`.

The unit tests pass because they use `#[tokio::test]` which provides a runtime context.

## Fix

Either:
1. Defer telemetry writer spawning until `worker.run()` is called (inside block_on)
2. Create the runtime earlier and wrap Worker::new() inside it
3. Use a sync channel writer instead of tokio::spawn for the telemetry drain

Option 1 is cleanest — add a `Telemetry::start()` method called from within the async context.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `needle run --workspace /tmp/test --agent claude-anthropic-sonnet` starts without panic
- Worker enters BOOTING → SELECTING state (may exit with EXHAUSTED if no beads, that's fine)
- All existing tests still pass
close_reason: Fixed by deferring telemetry writer spawning until inside the tokio runtime. Root cause: Telemetry constructors called tokio::spawn() during Worker::new(), but the tokio runtime was not created until after. Fix: added PendingWriter struct; constructors store pending state; new Telemetry::start() called from Worker::run() inside block_on() spawns the writer. Regression test telemetry_new_does_not_require_runtime added. All tests pass.

title: Skip occupied tmux session names when launching workers
description:
## Problem

When launching new workers via `needle run`, the `launch_workers()` function in `src/cli/mod.rs` iterates sequentially through the NATO alphabet and calls `launch_in_tmux()` without checking whether a tmux session with that name already exists. If a session like `needle-claude-alpha` is already running and a second `needle run --count 3` is issued, tmux errors out on collision.

## Desired Behavior

Before creating a tmux session, check `list_needle_sessions()` for existing sessions and skip occupied NATO identifiers. Specifically:

1. Call `list_needle_sessions()` at the start of `launch_workers()`
2. Build a set of occupied worker IDs by parsing existing session names (strip `needle-{agent}-` prefix)
3. When assigning NATO alphabet names, skip any that are already occupied
4. If all 26 NATO names are occupied, bail with a clear error
5. Log a warning when skipping an occupied name so the operator knows

For single-worker launches with `--identifier`, if the requested identifier is already in use, bail with an error rather than silently colliding.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `launch_workers()` calls `list_needle_sessions()` before iterating
- [ ] Occupied NATO names are skipped; next available name is used
- [ ] `--identifier <name>` fails with a clear error if that session already exists
- [ ] Warning logged for each skipped name
- [ ] Unit test covering the skip logic (mock session list)
- [ ] Integration test: launch 2 workers, then launch 2 more — all 4 get unique names
close_reason: All 5 child beads complete — session collision avoidance fully implemented and tested

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New function `occupied_worker_ids(agent: &str) -> Result<HashSet<String>>`

- [ ] Returns empty set if no sessions are running or tmux is unavailable

title: E2E: Heartbeat and peer monitoring — stale worker recovery
description:
## What

Prove that the Mend strand detects a crashed/dead worker's stale heartbeat, releases its claimed beads, and cleans up the worker registry entry.

## Test Plan

1. Create a workspace with 2 beads
2. Simulate a crashed worker:
   - Write a fake heartbeat file with last_heartbeat older than TTL (e.g., 10 minutes ago)
   - Write a fake worker registry entry with a PID that doesn't exist
   - Claim bead-1 with the fake worker's ID via `br update --claim`
3. Launch a real worker
4. Assert:
   - Mend strand detects the stale heartbeat (telemetry: mend.stale_heartbeat_detected)
   - Mend releases bead-1 from the dead worker (telemetry: mend.bead_released)
   - Mend removes the dead worker from the registry
   - Pluck then claims bead-1 (now available) and processes it
   - Both beads eventually CLOSED

## Why

Workers crash. SIGKILL happens. OOM killer happens. The system must self-heal without human intervention. This is Design Principle 5 (Self-healing).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stale heartbeat detected within one mend cycle
- Claimed beads released and reprocessable
- Dead worker cleaned from registry
- No manual intervention required
close_reason: E2E heartbeat/peer monitoring test added. Two-phase test proving Mend detects stale heartbeat, releases orphaned bead, cleans registry, and bead becomes reprocessable. 22 assertions pass. Also fixed MitosisConfig test compilation.

title: P2: Worker state registry
description:
## Task
Shared JSON file tracking all active workers for fleet-level awareness.

## Background
The registry enables needle list, needle status, and fleet-level telemetry.
It is NOT used for coordination — heartbeats handle that. The registry is
informational: "who is running, what are they doing, how many beads processed."

## Deliverables
- File: ~/.needle/state/workers.json

- Flock-protected read-modify-write (atomic updates)
- Fields: id, pid, workspace, agent, model, started_at, beads_processed

## Acceptance Criteria
- Worker appears in registry on start, disappears on clean stop
- Concurrent registration from multiple workers doesn't corrupt file
- needle list shows all registered workers
- Stale entries (from crashed workers) cleaned by Mend strand

title: P1: Integration tests and cross-platform build
description:
## Task
Write integration tests that verify the full Phase 1 system end-to-end,
and set up cross-platform builds for Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.

## Background
v1 tests mocked br output and missed that br show --json never included
labels — the mock was wrong, so all label-dependent logic was untested.
v2 integration tests run against a real br instance with a test .beads/
directory. This catches bugs at the tool boundary, not just in NEEDLE code.

## Deliverables

### Integration test: end-to-end single worker
- Create test .beads/ workspace with known beads
- Launch worker with mock agent (bash script that exits with specific code)
- Verify: bead claimed, prompt built, agent dispatched, outcome handled, telemetry logged
- Verify: worker loops to next bead

### Integration test: all 6 outcome paths
- Mock agents: exit 0, exit 1, exit 124, exit 127, exit 130 (SIGUSR1), timeout
- Verify each produces correct outcome classification and bead action
- Verify telemetry events match expected sequence

### Integration test: exhaustion
- Empty workspace → Pluck returns NoWork → Knot fires → EXHAUSTED
- Verify worker enters backoff and eventually exits (idle_timeout)

### Integration test: graceful shutdown
- Send SIGTERM during EXECUTING → verify bead released, worker exits cleanly
- Send SIGTERM during SELECTING → verify worker exits (no bead to release)

### Property test: deterministic ordering
- Generate random queue states
- Verify: two independent sort operations produce identical ordering

### Cross-platform builds
- GitHub Actions matrix: linux x86_64, macOS arm64
- cargo build --release --target for each
- Artifacts uploaded to release (or just CI verification for now)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All integration tests pass with real br (not mocked)
- Tests create and clean up their own .beads/ directories
- Tests do not interfere with each other (parallel-safe)
- CI builds succeed on both platforms
- Release binary is statically linked (musl on Linux)

## Dependencies
- All Phase 1 components complete

## Considerations
- Use cargo test --test integration_tests to separate from unit tests
- The mock agent should be a simple bash script in tests/fixtures/:
  tests/fixtures/mock-agent-success.sh (exit 0)
  tests/fixtures/mock-agent-failure.sh (exit 1)
  tests/fixtures/mock-agent-timeout.sh (sleep 999)
  etc.
- Consider using assert_cmd and predicates crates for CLI testing.
- For cross-compilation to macOS from Linux CI, use cross or
  cargo-zigbuild. Alternatively, use a macOS runner in GitHub Actions.
close_reason: Implemented 20 integration tests covering the full Phase 1 system end-to-end

title: E2E: Strand waterfall progression — Pluck → Mend → Explore → Knot
description:
## What

Prove that when the home workspace has no work, the worker correctly progresses through the strand waterfall: Pluck (empty) → Mend (nothing to clean) → Explore (finds work in another workspace) → back to Pluck with remote bead.

Also prove that when ALL strands return NoWork, the worker reaches Knot and then EXHAUSTED.

## Test Plan

### Scenario A: Explore finds remote work

1. Create home workspace (empty, no beads)
2. Create remote workspace with 1 bead
3. Configure explore.workspaces to include the remote workspace
4. Launch worker with home workspace
5. Assert:
   - Pluck returns NoWork (telemetry: strand.pluck.no_work)
   - Mend runs and returns NoWork (telemetry: strand.mend.no_work)
   - Explore finds the remote bead (telemetry: strand.explore.found with workspace path)
   - Worker claims and dispatches the remote bead
   - After completion, worker returns to home workspace for next cycle
   - Remote bead is CLOSED

### Scenario B: All strands empty → EXHAUSTED

1. Create home workspace (empty)
2. Configure no remote workspaces (or all remote also empty)
3. Launch worker
4. Assert:
   - Pluck → Mend → Explore → Knot all return NoWork
   - Worker reaches EXHAUSTED terminal state
   - Telemetry shows the full waterfall sequence
   - Clean exit code 0

## Acceptance Criteria
- Both scenarios pass
- Strand execution order matches plan spec (Pluck, Mend, Explore, Weave, Unravel, Pulse, Knot)
- Explore does NOT scan the filesystem (only configured paths)
- Worker returns to home workspace after remote bead execution
close_reason: E2E strand waterfall test implemented with Explore remote workspace fixes
